sanskrit_devanagari:
न मां कर्माणि लिम्पन्ति न मे कर्मफले स्पृहा ।
इति मां योऽभिजानाति कर्मभिर्न स बध्यते ॥ १४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
na māṁ karmāṇi limpanti na me karma-phale spṛhā |
iti māṁ yo 'bhijānāti karmabhir na sa badhyate || 14 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: There is no work that affects Me; nor do I aspire for the fruits of action. One who understands this truth about Me also does not become entangled in the fruitive reactions of work.
There is no work that affects Me; nor do I aspire for the fruits of action. One who understands this truth about Me also does not become entangled in the fruitive reactions of work.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 2967 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: As there are constitutional laws in the material world stating that the king can do no wrong, or that the king is not subject to the state laws, similarly the Lord, although He is the creator of this material world, is not affected by the activities of the material world.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.14 Because of the absence of egoism, those karmani, actions; na limpanti, do not taint; mam, Me, by becoming the originators of body etc. And me, for Me; na sprha, there is no hankering for the results of those actions.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-14-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the model: the Lord acts (creates the four varṇas, sustains the world) without being bound, because he has no spṛhā for fruits. The knower who recognises this mode of acting — and adopts it — is similarly unbound. Knowledge of the Lord's non-binding action becomes the practitioner's release.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.14 These actions of varied nature like creation etc., do not contaminate Me i.e., do not bind Me. For the distinctions of gods, men etc., are not brought about by Me, but by the particular Karmas, good and evil, of created beings.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-14-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as Krishna's self-revelation: he acts but is not bound because his action is performed without desire for fruits. The bhakta who knows this and acts in the same mode (without selfish desire, in service to the Lord) is also released from karmic binding.

positions.rendering: knows clearly enough to adopt the same mode of action
knows with the recognition that issues in devotion-shaped action

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three read abhijānāti as more than nominal recognition — it is a knowledge that becomes practice. Shankara emphasises the cognitive-imitative aspect (knowing how the Lord acts → acting that way). Ramanuja emphasises the relational aspect (knowing the Lord → devotion-shaped action). The verse's force is in the practical consequence, not the propositional content.

literal_meaning: Actions do not smear me; I have no longing for the fruits of action; whoever clearly knows me thus is not bound by actions.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names two facts: actions do not bind him (na limpanti), and he has no longing for fruits (na spṛhā). The verse then closes with the soteriological claim: whoever knows Krishna's mode of acting — engaged action without binding — is themselves freed from binding by their own actions. The verse models for the practitioner what right-action looks like: action without longing for fruits.
